combination.context_title: Modernismo Catalan / Borde de Canvas Close-up
combination.context_description: Selected visual world: modernismo_catalan | Camera slot: Borde de Canvas Close-up | Use only the selected world mother reference image as the environment source. | Inherit atmosphere from the selected world mother image. | Inherit materials from the selected world mother image. | Inherit lighting from the selected world mother image.
combination.compatibility_reason: User-selected scene mother category from the ranked list. Camera slots reconstruct this visual world from their own viewpoint.
combination.camera_slot_description:
Camera is close to the wall-mounted canvas edge, positioned at artwork height or slightly oblique to read the front face, lateral side, thickness, and shadow against the nearby wall.
Use a close-focus 50-70 mm lens equivalent or similar natural close-up lens that preserves plausible canvas thickness and avoids wide-angle exaggeration.
Keep vertical tilt controlled and material-oriented, enough to reveal edge depth and wall contact without making the canvas float or bend.
Use a close side-oblique angle across the canvas edge so the transition between painted front, lateral canvas depth, stretcher thickness, and wall shadow is visible.
Partial artwork view is allowed and expected. This is a camera crop, not an artwork alteration. The full artwork does not need to be visible, and one or more artwork edges may fall outside the final image frame. Close-up optical distortion and mild foreshortening are allowed if they come from the physical lens and edge perspective. The canvas edge and thickness are the subject. The artwork may be cropped by the camera frame, with wall texture, lateral side, subtle cast shadow, and the physical transition from front surface to side edge carrying the composition. Preserve the original artwork format even when only a fragment is visible: a portrait artwork must still read as a fragment of a taller-than-wide canvas, a landscape artwork as a fragment of a wider-than-tall canvas, and a square artwork as square. Do not complete, widen, compress, or redesign the visible fragment into a different full-format painting.
Use close-up photographic depth of field. Canvas edge, visible artwork portion, side depth, and immediate wall contact should remain sharp; distant wall texture may soften gently.
Camera object integrity: Physical canvas protection: edge thickness must remain moderate and plausible for the real artwork depth; it must look like stretched canvas, not poster, paper, screen, floating panel, or exaggerated block. Artwork proportion protection: the physical canvas keeps the source artwork aspect ratio and orientation even if only a partial close-up is shown. Artwork identity protection: preserve exact artwork identity on the visible portion. Anti-substitution protection: do not replace the artwork with another image.
Camera negatives: no poster, no flat print, no digital screen, no floating panel, no exaggerated thickness, no artwork substitution, no pets, no crowds, no children.
combination.final_prompt_preview:
Create a photorealistic premium art mockup using the provided artwork image as the exact authoritative visual reference.

The artwork image is the product. The room is only the premium human, cultural, architectural, seasonal, and material context that enriches the artwork’s presentation.

ARTWORK TECHNICAL DATA

Artwork root reference file: base_artwork_gemini_job_1782919651_4686_v1.png
Artwork physical width: 120 cm
Artwork physical height: 80 cm
Artwork physical depth: 2 cm
Artwork orientation: landscape

Use these physical dimensions as factual scale data. Do not reinterpret them as decorative suggestions.

ABSOLUTE ARTWORK FIDELITY

Recreate the provided artwork faithfully inside the mockup scene.

Preserve exactly:

* composition
* colors
* marks
* surface texture
* proportions
* orientation
* format
* visual identity
* physical presence

Do not repaint, redesign, crop, mirror, recolor, simplify, reinterpret, stylize, beautify, extend, repair, or modify the artwork.

The artwork must appear as a real physical canvas or real physical artwork installed in the space, not as a flat digital poster pasted onto a wall.

If canvas depth is visible, show believable physical thickness and side depth. The artwork must keep its real proportions and must not be warped.

SELECTED MOCKUP CONTEXT PROPOSAL

Use the following selected mockup context proposal as the authoritative scene, context, atmosphere, lighting, materials, scale, installation, and camera direction for this mockup:

MOCKUP CONTEXT PROPOSAL:
* World Mother Category: modernismo_catalan
* World Mother Reference Image: storage/world_mothers/modernismo_catalan/world_mother_20260701_152125_7448_04.png
* Mode: direct world mother plus selected camera slot; no artwork analysis, title, description, curatorial reading, or context ranking was used.
* Space Type: Modernismo Catalan visual world reconstructed from the supplied reference.
* Materials: Objects, materials, furniture family, wall language, floor language, and lighting should be inferred from the supplied world mother reference image without preserving its source photo layout.
* Lighting: Use the lighting character visible in the supplied world mother image; do not invent a conflicting time of day or unrelated gallery lighting.
* Placement: wall_hanging
* Camera View: Selected camera slot viewpoint: Borde de Canvas Close-up.
* Camera Group: borde_canvas_closeup
* Camera Distance: Use the distance required by the selected camera slot. The world mother image provides visual DNA for the environment, but not the camera position, layout, crop, or perspective.
* Camera Notes: The camera slot is authoritative for viewpoint, lens, crop, height, and perspective. The world mother image supplies environment identity, object vocabulary, material language, palette, and lighting, but the room must be rebuilt from the selected camera viewpoint rather than preserving the source photo layout. DETAIL CAMERA CROP OVERRIDE: for this canvas-edge close-up, camera-frame cropping is allowed and expected. The final image may show only a physical slice of the artwork face plus the side edge, thickness, wall contact, and cast shadow. This is not permission to alter the artwork itself: preserve the real canvas orientation, real aspect ratio, real colors, and visible local composition. If the source artwork is portrait, the visible fragment must still feel like part of a taller-than-wide canvas, not a complete square painting.
* Camera Slot ID: borde_canvas_closeup
* Camera Slot: Borde de Canvas Close-up
* Camera Slot Geometry:
Camera is close to the wall-mounted canvas edge, positioned at artwork height or slightly oblique to read the front face, lateral side, thickness, and shadow against the nearby wall.
Use a close-focus 50-70 mm lens equivalent or similar natural close-up lens that preserves plausible canvas thickness and avoids wide-angle exaggeration.
Keep vertical tilt controlled and material-oriented, enough to reveal edge depth and wall contact without making the canvas float or bend.
Use a close side-oblique angle across the canvas edge so the transition between painted front, lateral canvas depth, stretcher thickness, and wall shadow is visible.
Partial artwork view is allowed and expected. This is a camera crop, not an artwork alteration. The full artwork does not need to be visible, and one or more artwork edges may fall outside the final image frame. Close-up optical distortion and mild foreshortening are allowed if they come from the physical lens and edge perspective. The canvas edge and thickness are the subject. The artwork may be cropped by the camera frame, with wall texture, lateral side, subtle cast shadow, and the physical transition from front surface to side edge carrying the composition. Preserve the original artwork format even when only a fragment is visible: a portrait artwork must still read as a fragment of a taller-than-wide canvas, a landscape artwork as a fragment of a wider-than-tall canvas, and a square artwork as square. Do not complete, widen, compress, or redesign the visible fragment into a different full-format painting.
Use close-up photographic depth of field. Canvas edge, visible artwork portion, side depth, and immediate wall contact should remain sharp; distant wall texture may soften gently.
Camera object integrity: Physical canvas protection: edge thickness must remain moderate and plausible for the real artwork depth; it must look like stretched canvas, not poster, paper, screen, floating panel, or exaggerated block. Artwork proportion protection: the physical canvas keeps the source artwork aspect ratio and orientation even if only a partial close-up is shown. Artwork identity protection: preserve exact artwork identity on the visible portion. Anti-substitution protection: do not replace the artwork with another image.
Camera negatives: no poster, no flat print, no digital screen, no floating panel, no exaggerated thickness, no artwork substitution, no pets, no crowds, no children.
* Mockup Combination Notes: Combination preview uses the root artwork image plus the selected real world mother reference image and the selected camera slot.
* Human Presence: none unless required by the selected camera slot.
* Mockup Prompt: Use the supplied world mother reference image as visual DNA for the environment: storage/world_mothers/modernismo_catalan/world_mother_20260701_152125_7448_04.png. Keep its recognizable room identity, object vocabulary, color palette, materials, furniture family, wall language, floor language, and lighting character. Rebuild the environment through the viewpoint required by the selected camera slot; the reference photo camera angle, layout, crop, and perspective are not binding. Scene mother variant: opposite #8. Use this as the opposite/complementary scene mother variant, chosen to support reversed room dominance and alternate orbital camera readings while preserving the same world. Camera role: build "Borde de Canvas Close-up" as a new camera construction using the same visual DNA. The mother reference supplies environment identity, object vocabulary, material language, palette, wall/floor language, lighting, and furnishing family; it does not supply the final camera angle, layout, crop, height, or perspective. DETAIL CAMERA CROP OVERRIDE: for this canvas-edge close-up, camera-frame cropping is allowed and expected. The final image may show only a physical slice of the artwork face plus the side edge, thickness, wall contact, and cast shadow. This is not permission to alter the artwork itself: preserve the real canvas orientation, real aspect ratio, real colors, and visible local composition. If the source artwork is portrait, the visible fragment must still feel like part of a taller-than-wide canvas, not a complete square painting.
* Negative Prompt: no unrelated gallery, no white cube gallery unless present in the world mother image, no generic showroom, no replacement room style, no different furniture family, no different wall color family, no invented minimalist museum, no frozen copy of the source photo composition; camera-frame crop allowed for selected detail slot; no changed artwork format; no squared portrait artwork; no stretched artwork; no compressed artwork; no artwork substitution; no invented composition

The selected context proposal may contain deterministic camera fields assigned by the system. When present, these camera fields override looser or conflicting camera wording from the contextual scene description.

Do not ignore this context block. It defines the specific mockup direction to generate.

PRIMARY IMAGE PRIORITIES

1. Artistic camera handling is the first priority.
   The camera must actively build the image. It must create depth, elegance, product presence, spatial credibility, and commercial strength.

2. Premium human-cultural context is the second priority.
   The environment must feel like a specific human world: cultural, architectural, historical, seasonal, studio-based, or collector-oriented. Avoid generic interiors.

3. The context must fit the artwork.
   The space, materials, atmosphere, light, and camera must respond to the artwork’s colors, rhythm, density, silence, contrast, symbolic tension, or emotional tone.

4. The final mockup must enrich the artwork.
   The result must feel more valuable, more specific, more visually rich, and more commercially powerful than a simple artwork-on-wall mockup.

SCENE AND CONTEXT RULES

Use the selected mockup proposal as the main scene direction.

The context must be visually specific. Avoid vague vintage luxury, generic beige rooms, hotel-like decoration, real-estate render interiors, or repeated warm stock interiors.

If the scene is antique, make it authentically old-world.
If the scene is modern, make it clearly contemporary.
If the scene is Mediterranean, use refined southern European architecture, mineral walls, limewash, stone, travertine, terracotta, linen, filtered sunlight, shutters, or summer air.
If the scene is Eastern or Oriental-inspired, make it premium, restrained, respectful, and architecturally refined. Avoid exotic clichés.
If the scene is historical, express the period through architecture, furniture, materials, light, and atmosphere, not theatrical props.
If the scene is Renaissance, Belle Époque, or 1940s Paris, keep it believable, elegant, and premium.
If the scene is a collector interior, make it specific, lived, cultivated, and visually controlled, not generic.

Do not default to the same beige room, brown furniture, vague vintage armchair, soft natural light, or winter atmosphere.

SEASONAL CONTEXT RULES

Use seasonal awareness only when compatible with the selected context proposal.

Europe and North America are the main buying markets. If the current season is spring or summer, avoid making the scene feel completely wintery, dim, closed, heavy, or brown unless the selected proposal explicitly requires a moody interior.

Seasonal cues may include:

* clearer daylight
* refined summer light
* Mediterranean sun filtered through curtains or shutters
* breathable interior atmosphere
* lighter mineral materials
* soft seasonal freshness
* controlled brightness with premium contrast

Do not turn the scene into a vacation postcard. Keep it collector-grade, elegant, architectural, and commercially refined.

LIGHTING RULES

Lighting must be intentional and must create visual contrast.

Use one clear lighting strategy:

* Mediterranean directional sunlight
* soft summer daylight
* premium gallery spotlight
* moody evening collector light
* chiaroscuro historical light
* cool architectural daylight
* dark wall with focused artwork illumination
* warm interior light contrasted with deeper shadows

Avoid flat ambient lighting. Avoid generic evenly lit rooms. Avoid six similar warm beige interiors.

CAMERA RULES

The selected camera direction is mandatory.

The camera must not make the artwork look like a poster. Even when the selected proposal is frontal, the artwork must still feel like a physical object with real material presence, believable wall contact, subtle depth, and natural perspective.

Allowed camera directions:

* frontal physical editorial view
* near-frontal subtle depth view
* soft left-oblique view
* soft right-oblique view
* controlled low 3/4 view
* artistic stronger 3/4 or 7/8 view
* elevated 3/4 architectural view
* controlled high-angle architectural view
* controlled low-angle or contrapicado view
* low floor wide low-angle view only when explicitly selected

Avoid:

* flat pasted poster effect
* fisheye
* extreme wide angle
* distorted architecture
* surveillance-like top-down view
* uncontrolled aerial view
* uncontrolled floor-level distortion
* product scale distortion

If using high-angle, aerial, low-angle, contrapicado, or low floor wide low-angle views, preserve believable architecture, furniture scale, wall geometry, and artwork proportions. Do not flatten these views into a generic frontal close-up.

If using a low-angle or contrapicado view, keep it controlled. The camera may sit slightly below the artwork centerline, but not so low that furniture scale becomes unbelievable.

SCALE AND COMPOSITION RULES

The artwork must be the primary subject, but its scale must remain physically believable.

Respect the artwork’s real physical dimensions:

* width: 120 cm
* height: 80 cm
* depth: 2 cm
* orientation: landscape

The artwork must be coherent in relation to:

* sofas
* chairs
* consoles
* easels
* doors
* paneling
* ceiling height
* floor level
* architectural features

Do not enlarge the artwork unrealistically just to dominate the frame.
Do not shrink the artwork until it loses commercial impact.

The mockup should feel like a premium product photograph, not a decorative background image.

The artwork should occupy a strong visual position in the composition, usually close or medium-close, with enough surrounding architecture to create context and atmosphere.

PHYSICAL INSTALLATION RULES

The artwork may be:

* mounted on a wall
* leaning against a wall
* displayed on an easel
* installed in a collector room
* placed in a refined studio context
* integrated into a premium architectural interior

The installation must be physically plausible.

If mounted, it should cast subtle realistic shadows.
If leaning, it must have correct contact with floor and wall.
If on an easel, the easel must support the artwork believably.
If shown at an angle, the canvas side depth must be visible and coherent.

HUMAN CONTEXT WITHOUT VISIBLE HUMANS

Do not include visible people, human figures, silhouettes, faces, hands, bodies, mannequins, animals, pets, birds, or insects.

Human context must be expressed through:

* architecture
* furniture
* books
* collector objects
* studio tools
* historical period
* cultural design language
* seasonal light
* materials
* lived refinement
* traces of human taste

Do not add theatrical props, costumes, or narrative figures.

STYLE AND MATERIAL QUALITY

The mockup must look photorealistic, premium, editorial, and commercially usable.

Use high-quality materials when appropriate to the selected context:

* limewash
* mineral plaster
* stone
* travertine
* marble
* terracotta
* dark wood
* pale oak
* walnut
* bronze
* black metal
* linen
* paper screens
* lacquer
* aged European walls
* refined paneling
* architectural concrete
* polished or matte floors

Avoid cheap furniture, generic stock interiors, fake luxury, oversaturated decoration, clutter, unrealistic reflections, and AI-looking objects.

CONTEXT NEGATIVE PROMPT

Apply the following negative prompt from the selected mockup context when present:

no unrelated gallery, no white cube gallery unless present in the world mother image, no generic showroom, no replacement room style, no different furniture family, no different wall color family, no invented minimalist museum, no frozen copy of the source photo composition; camera-frame crop allowed for selected detail slot; no changed artwork format; no squared portrait artwork; no stretched artwork; no compressed artwork; no artwork substitution; no invented composition

GLOBAL NEGATIVE RULES

No visible people.
No animals.
No pets.
No birds.
No insects.
No human silhouettes.
No mannequins.
No poster effect.
No flat pasted artwork.
No altered artwork.
No cropped artwork.
No mirrored artwork.
No changed colors.
No redesigned composition.
No fake frame unless explicitly requested.
No incorrect canvas proportions.
No distorted perspective.
No fisheye.
No warped architecture.
No unrealistic artwork scale.
No excessive wide-angle view.
No generic beige vintage room.
No repeated vague warm luxury interior.
No winter default unless explicitly intended.
No decorative clichés.
No theatrical historical scene.
No exotic clichés.
No real-estate render look.
No hotel lobby look.
No Pinterest-style decoration.
No cheap furniture.
No clutter.
No text, watermark, signature overlay, logo, UI elements, or labels.

FINAL RESULT

Generate a premium photorealistic art mockup where the artwork remains exact, physical, believable, and commercially powerful.

The image must be visually enriched by artistic camera handling, specific human-cultural context, seasonal awareness, strong material quality, and a meaningful relationship between the artwork and the environment.

ARTWORK SCALE POLICY

This block is the only authority for artwork size. Camera slots may control viewpoint, lens, tilt, rotation, crop, and composition, but they must not redefine artwork scale.

Physical artwork size: 120 cm wide x 80 cm high x 2 cm deep.
Resolved size class: medium.
Resolved orientation: landscape.

Keep the artwork at its real physical size inside the room. Do not reinterpret it as XL, monumental, billboard-sized, wall-filling, architectural panel-sized, stage-prop-sized, or mural-scale unless these supplied dimensions truly support that reading.

The artwork height must read below a typical 205 cm interior door when a door or comparable architectural height is visible.
If an extreme camera angle makes the artwork less dominant at true scale, keep the true scale and allow the room/camera composition to carry more of the image. Do not enlarge the artwork just to keep it visually dominant.

Scale must be inferred from architecture, furniture, floor/wall junctions, windows, ceiling height, and installation contact. These references must adapt to the artwork's real size; the artwork must not be enlarged to satisfy camera drama.

ARTWORK PAINTED EDGE POLICY

When a canvas side edge, corner, lateral depth, or stretcher thickness is visible, that visible edge must be painted as a physical continuation of the artwork.

The side edge must carry wrapped color, pigment, texture, brush rhythm, marks, and material energy from the nearest front-face area. It should feel like the painting continues around the canvas depth, not like a blank neutral canvas edge.

Do not show raw beige canvas, unpainted fabric, white primer, bare wood, exposed stretcher bars, a blank side band, or a generic neutral lateral strip unless the source artwork itself explicitly shows that kind of edge.

The edge continuation must remain local and coherent: extend nearby color fields and texture around the side, but do not invent a new composition, new symbols, new figures, new text, or unrelated imagery on the side edge.

The front face remains the identity authority. The edge is a wrapped physical continuation of the nearest visible artwork surface, not a separate painting.

ARTWORK DETAIL CROP POLICY

Detail camera slots are allowed to crop the camera frame, not the physical artwork. The artwork may continue outside the final image boundary. Do not scale down, shorten, squash, reformat, or redesign the artwork so the whole object fits inside a close-up detail image.

For landscape artwork, the visible area must read as a cropped window onto a wider canvas that continues beyond the camera frame. Do not narrow the artwork, compress its width, make it portrait, square it, or show it as a complete compact panel just to fit the detail view.

If the camera is close to an edge, corner, or surface, show only the needed physical slice. It is better for the artwork to leave the frame than for the artwork to become a smaller or differently proportioned object.

Top, bottom, left, or right artwork boundaries may be outside the generated image. Missing boundaries must be understood as camera crop, not as a shorter artwork.

WORLD MOTHER AUTHORITY POLICY - DETAIL CAMERA

The selected camera slot remains the highest authority for composition, crop, lens behavior, camera height, tilt, distance, and perspective.

This is a detail/material camera. The world mother image is still a visual reference, but only for peripheral environment identity: light quality, wall or floor color, material atmosphere, studio palette, distant blurred hints, and believable surrounding context.

Do not let windows, furniture, easels, tables, shelves, props, architecture, or a full-room layout compete with the detail camera. The artwork surface, edge, pigment relief, canvas weave, painted side continuation, and shallow optical depth must remain dominant.

The world mother reference must not make the generator show the whole artwork, pull the camera backward, widen the composition into a room scene, or replace the selected detail camera with a general atelier mockup.

SELECTED DETAIL CAMERA OVERRIDE

This selected camera slot intentionally allows camera-frame cropping of the artwork. This overrides generic "do not crop", "no cropped artwork", and "show the whole artwork" instructions only for photographic framing.

Do not crop, resize, repaint, extend, redesign, or alter the artwork itself. The central ARTWORK SCALE POLICY owns the true physical size and orientation; this detail camera only controls photographic framing of a faithful fragment.

Because the artwork is landscape, any visible canvas fragment must still feel like part of a wider-than-tall physical artwork. Do not make it portrait, square it, compress its width, or complete the visible fragment into a different format.

For Borde de Canvas Close-up, prioritize the physical side edge, canvas thickness, wall contact, cast shadow, and a faithful partial slice of the painted face. The whole artwork should usually not be visible.
